examples.title: First Come First Served (FCFS) Scheduling
examples.description: Implementation of non-preemptive FCFS scheduling algorithm
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS_Scheduling
INPUT: Array of processes with arrival time and burst time
OUTPUT: Average waiting time and turnaround time

BEGIN
  READ n  // number of processes
  FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
    READ processes[i].arrival_time
    READ processes[i].burst_time
    processes[i].process_id = i + 1
  END FOR

  // Sort processes by arrival time
  Sort processes by arrival_time ascending

  current_time = 0
  total_waiting_time = 0
  total_turnaround_time = 0

  FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
    IF current_time < processes[i].arrival_time THEN
      current_time = processes[i].arrival_time
    END IF

    // Calculate waiting time
    waiting_time = current_time - processes[i].arrival_time
    total_waiting_time = total_waiting_time + waiting_time

    // Execute process
    current_time = current_time + processes[i].burst_time

    // Calculate turnaround time
    turnaround_time = current_time - processes[i].arrival_time
    total_turnaround_time = total_turnaround_time + turnaround_time
  END FOR

  avg_waiting_time = total_waiting_time / n
  avg_turnaround_time = total_turnaround_time / n

  PRINT "Average Waiting Time:", avg_waiting_time
  PRINT "Average Turnaround Time:", avg_turnaround_time
END
examples.explanation: FCFS schedules processes in the order of their arrival time. The algorithm is simple but may lead to the convoy effect where short processes wait for long CPU-bound processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for sorting + O(n) for scheduling = O(n log n)

examples.title: Round Robin Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of preemptive Round Robin time-sharing scheduling
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Round_Robin_Scheduling
INPUT: Array of processes, time_quantum
OUTPUT: Average waiting time and turnaround time

BEGIN
  READ n, time_quantum
  FOR i = 0 to n-1 DO
    READ processes[i].arrival_time
    READ processes[i].burst_time
    processes[i].remaining_time = processes[i].burst_time
    processes[i].process_id = i + 1
  END FOR

  ready_queue = empty queue
  current_time = 0
  completed = 0
  total_waiting_time = 0

  // Add all processes arriving at time 0
  FOR each process P DO
    IF P.arrival_time == 0 THEN
      Add P to ready_queue
    END IF
  END FOR

  WHILE completed < n DO
    IF ready_queue is empty THEN
      current_time = find earliest arriving process
      Add processes arriving at current_time to ready_queue
    END IF

    // Get next process from ready queue
    current_process = Remove from front of ready_queue

    // Execute for time quantum or until completion
    execution_time = minimum(time_quantum, current_process.remaining_time)
    current_process.remaining_time = current_process.remaining_time - execution_time
    current_time = current_time + execution_time

    // Add newly arrived processes to ready queue
    FOR each process P DO
      IF P.arrival_time <= current_time AND P not in ready_queue AND P.remaining_time > 0 THEN
        Add P to ready_queue
      END IF
    END FOR

    IF current_process.remaining_time > 0 THEN
      // Process not completed, add back to queue
      Add current_process to rear of ready_queue
    ELSE
      // Process completed
      turnaround_time = current_time - current_process.arrival_time
      waiting_time = turnaround_time - current_process.burst_time
      total_waiting_time = total_waiting_time + waiting_time
      completed = completed + 1
    END IF
  END WHILE

  avg_waiting_time = total_waiting_time / n
  PRINT "Average Waiting Time:", avg_waiting_time
END
examples.explanation: Round Robin provides fair time-sharing by giving each process a fixed time quantum. It ensures no process Starves for CPU time and provides good response time for interactive systems.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × burst_time / quantum) - depends on number of context switches
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for process storage and queue

examples.title: Shortest Job First (SJF) Non-Preemptive
examples.description: Python implementation of non-preemptive SJF scheduling
examples.code:
def sjf_scheduling(processes):
    """
    processes: list of tuples (arrival_time, burst_time, process_id)
    Returns: average waiting time and turnaround time
    """
    n = len(processes)
    
    # Create process objects
    proc_list = []
    for i, (at, bt) in enumerate(processes):
        proc_list.append({
            'id': i + 1,
            'arrival': at,
            'burst': bt,
            'remaining': bt
        })
    
    # Sort by arrival time initially
    proc_list.sort(key=lambda x: x['arrival'])
    
    completed = 0
    current_time = 0
    total_waiting = 0
    total_turnaround = 0
    ready_queue = []
    completed_processes = []
    
    while completed < n:
        # Add processes that have arrived
        for p in proc_list:
            if p['arrival'] <= current_time and p not in ready_queue and p not in completed_processes:
                ready_queue.append(p)
        
        if ready_queue:
            # Select process with shortest burst time
            ready_queue.sort(key=lambda x: x['burst'])
            current_proc = ready_queue.pop(0)
            
            # Calculate waiting time
            waiting_time = current_time - current_proc['arrival']
            total_waiting += waiting_time
            
            # Execute process
            current_time += current_proc['burst']
            
            # Calculate turnaround time
            turnaround = current_time - current_proc['arrival']
            total_turnaround += turnaround
            
            completed_processes.append(current_proc)
            completed += 1
        else:
            # Move to next arrival time
            current_time = min(p['arrival'] for p in proc_list if p not in completed_processes)
    
    avg_waiting = total_waiting / n
    avg_turnaround = total_turnaround / n
    
    return avg_waiting, avg_turnaround

# Example usage
processes = [(0, 8), (1, 4), (2, 2), (3, 6)]
# Process IDs: P1, P2, P3, P4
avg_wait, avg_tat = sjf_scheduling(processes)
print(f"Average Waiting Time: {avg_wait}")
print(f"Average Turnaround Time: {avg_tat}")
examples.explanation: SJF selects the process with the shortest burst time from the ready queue. It provides optimal average waiting time but requires knowledge of burst times in advance.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) due to sorting at each selection step
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing processes and queue

examples.title: Priority Scheduling (Preemptive)
examples.description: C implementation of preemptive priority-based CPU scheduling
examples.explanation: Preemptive priority scheduling gives the CPU to the highest priority process. Lower number = higher priority in this implementation. It can cause starvation of lower priority processes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × total_burst_time) due to preemptive nature
